---
title: Regular expressions in stringr
date: 2026-03-25
draft: true
editor_options: 
  chunk_output_type: console
execute: 
  echo: true
  eval: true
---


```{r}
#| echo: false
here::i_am("r_training/stringr_regex.qmd")
library(here)

hl <- function(text, regex){
  # function to highlight regex-matched text in light green
  # not useful here, but will be great for the regex in R session
  
  result_string <- stringr::str_replace_all(text, paste0("(", regex, ")"), paste0("<span style='background-color:lightgreen'>", "\\1", "</span>"))
  
  regex_string = paste0("`", gsub("\\\\", "\\", regex, fixed = T), "`")
  
  tibble::tibble(regex = c("input", "result"),
                !!regex_string := c(text, result_string))  |>
  knitr::kable()
  
}
```


## A reminder about regular expressions

Here's some sample data for this session:

```{r}
#| echo: false
trial_names <- readr::read_rds(here::here("r_training/src/r_regex.rds"))

trial_names |>
  knitr::kable()
```

It contains a number of different health and care organisation names. In this session, we'll use regular expressions to find, extract, and replace pieces of text.

Please [download the rds file](src/r_regex.rds) and import into R. While there are several ways of doing that, a base-R solution would be something like the following:

```{r}
#| eval: false

utils::download.file("https://nes-dew.github.io/KIND-training/r_training/src/r_regex.rds", destfile = "r_regex.rds")
trial_names <- readRDS("r_regex.rds")

```


There are several ways of using regular expressions in R. The standard/classic/base-R way would be to use something like `grep`:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| results: markup

grep("NHS", trial_names$names) # indexes
grepl("NHS", trial_names$names) # logical
```

`grepl` and co are a bit messy to use, because they don't pipe nicely (their first argument isn't the data). You can use them directly inside some tidyverse functions as-is:

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true

trial_names |>
  dplyr::filter(grepl("NHS", names)) # with dplyr::filter
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis

trial_names |>
  dplyr::filter(grepl("NHS", names))  |> # with dplyr::filter
  knitr::kable()
```

But trying to use them in a pipe means using some kind of anonymous function:

```{r}
#| eval: true
#| echo: true
#| results: markup

trial_names$names |>
  sort() |>
  (\(x) grepl("NHS", x))()
```


## Stringr

A neater alternative (and the focus of this session) is to use the `stringr` package:

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| results: markup

library(stringr)

stringr::str_detect(trial_names$names, "NHS") # effectively grepl but pipe-able
```

```{r}
#| eval: false
#| echo: true

trial_names |>
  dplyr::filter(str_detect(names, "NHS"))
```

```{r}
#| echo: false
#| results: asis
trial_names |>
  dplyr::filter(str_detect(names, "NHS")) |>
  knitr::kable()
```

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| results: markup

stringr::str_extract(trial_names$names, "NHS") # as vector
```

One R-specific gotcha: any regex that contains a `\` needs doubling in R - so `\w` becomes `\\w` etc.

```{r}
#| echo: true
#| results: markup

stringr::str_extract(trial_names$names, "^\\w*")
```

## Validate some CHIs



## Groups

So far, our regular expressions have just matched a piece of text, and then selected either that piece of text (as the early examples in this session) or selected an entire word/line containing that match. Regular expression can also work in a more sophisticated way, where we can specify a piece of text to match, and also (independently) specify a piece of text to select. To do that selection, we'll use the idea of groups. Groups are formulated using round brackets - so anything contained within `()` will be selected, assuming any of the regex outwith those brackets is matched. 

:::{.callout-tip collapse=false appearance='default' icon=true}
## Task
- try the regex `NHS (GG&C)`
:::

![Groups example to match GG&C only when it follows NHS](src/images/groups_nhs.png)

That should select only `GG&C` when it follows NHS with a space. In other words, `NHS (GG&C)` is matched, but only the `GG&C` part is selected. We could tweak that to also pick up the final string in our test pool with a bit of quantification:

:::{.callout-tip collapse=false appearance='default' icon=true}
## Task
- try the regex `NHS *(GG&*C)`
:::

![Adding quantification to match GGC with and without spaces](src/images/groups_quant.png)



## Ack
Participants in the earlier regex session for suggestions about parsing/validating numbers
